ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Lachha Paratha exists; closest defensible match is the generic flatbread class FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Lachha Paratha; A000E (flatbread category) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Lachha Paratha or Indian flatbread at the required specificity level.

preparation_method: layered by fan-folding and coiling technique

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Lachha Paratha piece weighs approximately 120 g and provides around 350 kcal, making it a substantial accompaniment to a curry-based meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides compositional data for whole wheat flour (atta) and ghee-based preparations. Lachha Paratha values are derived from the atta paratha entry adjusted for the higher ghee content characteristic of the layered preparation. Micronutrient values for phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, and B-vitamins are consistent with ICMR-NIN whole wheat flour data. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole wheat flour and ghee) to validate the ghee contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for whole wheat chapati/paratha
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Lachha Paratha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 58 (medium) is estimated by analogy with whole wheat chapati/paratha entries (GI range 52–62) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The higher fat content from ghee moderates the glycemic response relative to plain whole wheat bread. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 38.0) / 100 = 22.0 for 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~292 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Lachha Paratha to one piece per meal and avoid pairing with high-sodium gravies, as the combined sodium load may exceed recommended daily limits; choosing a minimal-ghee preparation can reduce saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and moderate carbohydrate density (~32 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Lachha Paratha in controlled portions (one piece, ~120 g) paired with high-fibre legumes or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the whole wheat base and fat content from ghee moderately slow glucose absorption compared to refined-flour alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.42 g/100 g) from ghee used in layering and frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Lachha Paratha consumption due to its saturated fat and trans fat content from ghee; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to one piece and frequency limited to occasional meals, with preference given to preparations using minimal ghee or a plant-oil substitute.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Gluten content and high saturated fat, both potentially pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their response to Lachha Paratha, as gluten and saturated fats from ghee may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals; a gluten-free flatbread alternative with reduced fat is preferable for those with confirmed gluten sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lachha Paratha is a classic North Indian multi-layered flatbread originating from the Punjab region of India. The name 'Lachha' means 'rings' or 'strands' in Hindi, referring to the distinct concentric flaky layers that characterise this bread. It is traditionally made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, salt, and a generous quantity of ghee (clarified butter). The unique texture is achieved through a specific technique: the dough is rolled thin, brushed with ghee, pleated accordion-style, coiled into a spiral, and then rolled out again before being cooked on a tawa (iron griddle) with additional ghee until both sides are golden brown and the layers separate into crispy, flaky strands. The result is a rich, buttery flatbread with a crisp exterior and soft, layered interior. Lachha Paratha is a popular accompaniment to rich, gravy-based dishes such as butter chicken, dal makhani, and paneer butter masala, and is commonly served at breakfast, lunch, and dinner in North Indian households and restaurants. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 292 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 15 g fat (7.5 g saturated), and 31.7 g carbohydrates with 2.5 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), moderated by the fat content from ghee. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans, contains gluten and dairy, and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and caloric density.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Lachha Paratha?
What is the difference between Lachha Paratha and regular paratha?

meal_context: dinner with rich North Indian gravies

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for whole wheat atta-based paratha preparations with ghee; no exact Lachha Paratha entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. The GI is an analogous estimate from whole wheat chapati/paratha entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact ghee quantity used in layering varies by preparation, affecting fat, saturated fat, and caloric values.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Lachha Paratha prepared with ghee is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2024 edition) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the required specificity for this preparation. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: whole wheat flour and ghee contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. 4. Glycemic load per serving (120 g): GL = (58 × 38.0) / 100 = 22.0. The previous GL calculation was based on an incorrect serving size; it has been corrected to reflect the 120g serving size. 5. Portions: duplicate entries in the input were consolidated; two distinct portion sizes are recognised — 120 g (1 piece, restaurant/GrabFood reference) and 80 g (1 serving, enriched_json reference). The default serving used for GL calculation is 120 g. 6. Trans fat of 0.42 g/100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) present in ghee, not industrially produced partially hydrogenated oils. 7. Sodium of 291.67 mg/100 g is sourced from the enriched_json pipeline and is plausible for a salted, ghee-fried flatbread; reviewers should verify against a direct ICMR-NIN or HPB measurement if available.
